architecture.summary: This is a survey paper reviewing multi-step reasoning approaches with large language models. It does not propose a single system but rather categorizes existing approaches through a taxonomy of three stages: (1) step generation via prompts (hand-written, external knowledge, or model-generated), (2) step evaluation (self-assessment, tool-based, or external model validation), and (3) control of reasoning (greedy, ensemble, tree-search, or reinforcement learning). Information flows from prompt engineering to LLM inference, with optional external tools and verifiers providing feedback loops.

biblio.title: Multi-Step Reasoning with Large Language Models, a Survey

claims.statement: Chain-of-thought improved accuracy from 15.6% to 46.9% on GSM8K
Zero-Shot CoT achieved 70% accuracy on GSM8K with PaLM

Multi-step reasoning approaches have progressed beyond math word problems and can now successfully solve challenges in logic, combinatorial games, and robotics, sometimes by first generating code that is then executed by external tools
Many studies in multi-step methods use reinforcement learning for finetuning, external optimization loops, in-context reinforcement learning, and self-reflection
In-context learning occurs at inference time in language models beyond hundreds of billions of parameters, enabling few-shot learning without parameter updates
Scratchpad approach on polynomial evaluation improved from 32% to 51% accuracy with supervised learning of intermediate steps
The taxonomy identifies three stages of multi-step reasoning: step generation, step evaluation, and control of reasoning steps, with each stage having multiple sub-approaches
Traditional transformer-based LLMs designed for next-token prediction do not perform well on basic reasoning benchmarks without explicit multi-step prompting
